title: seanpatrickmay.github.io
oneLiner: Next.js portfolio with live Garmin training stats, Spotify feeds, and 16 project case studies—statically exported to GitHub Pages.
cardDescription: Statically exported Next.js portfolio with live Garmin and Spotify data widgets, filterable project archive, and per-project case study pages.
proofPoints: 16 project case-study pages generated from a structured JSON catalog
Scheduled scripts refresh Garmin activity stats and Spotify listening data into static JSON
Filterable project archive with search, multi-tag filters, and shareable URL query params
overview: This site is a static-exported Next.js portfolio that combines hand-curated project writeups with lightweight live data feeds. The goal is a fast personal site that still feels current without depending on a permanent backend.
whatIBuilt: Static-exported Next.js site with homepage, project archive, and per-project routes
Data refresh scripts and workflows for Garmin and Spotify summaries
Reusable UI primitives for cards, pills, filters, and long-form project pages
howItWorks: Scheduled scripts write JSON data into public/
Pages import static JSON directly during build time
GitHub Pages serves the exported site as static HTML
results: Fast static site with dynamic-feeling activity widgets
Shareable /projects/ + /projects/<slug>/ routes compatible with static export
nextSteps: Replace manual project metadata entry with a GitHub-informed sync path
Add screenshots and richer evidence to more project pages
bullets: 16 project case-study pages with cover images, proof points, galleries, and deep-dive sections.
Live Garmin training stats (8-week rolling window, 60 recent activities) and Spotify top-10 via scheduled refresh scripts.
Project archive with full-text search, multi-tag/language filters, and sort by featured ranking, date, or title.

title: Life-Dashboard
oneLiner: Full-stack wellness app with Garmin biometric ingestion, 20+ nutrient tracking, and OpenAI-driven readiness insights rendered in a Monet-inspired pixel art interface.
featuredDescription: Wellness dashboard turning Garmin biometrics + nutrition logs into AI-driven daily insights.
cardDescription: Garmin biometrics, nutrition tracking, and AI readiness insights in a Monet pixel art UI.
proofPoints: Async Garmin ingestion pipeline for HRV, resting HR, sleep, training load, and energy data
17 API routers, 35 React components, 18 database migrations, and 110+ pixel art sprite assets
OpenAI Responses API generates structured readiness narratives with web search and tool routing
overview: Life-Dashboard is a self-hosted wellness app that ingests Garmin HRV, resting heart rate, sleep, and training load data, tracks 20+ nutrients across meals, and generates AI-driven readiness insights—all rendered in a pixel art Monet theme that shifts with time of day.
whatIBuilt: FastAPI backend for Garmin ingestion, insight generation, and structured wellness workflows
React/Vite frontend for dashboard views, trends, nutrition, and assistant-style interaction
Postgres-backed data model for biometrics, nutrition logs, insights, and to-do generation
howItWorks: Pull Garmin activities and recovery metrics into Postgres
Aggregate recent trends and generate readiness-style summaries with OpenAI
Surface metrics, recommendations, and nutrition context through a React interface
results: End-to-end wellness pipeline from raw biometrics to actionable outputs
Clean separation between ingestion, modeling, and product UI
nextSteps: Add tighter evals for AI recommendations
Expand training-readiness logic into more personalized planning
bullets: Async Garmin pipeline ingests HRV, resting HR, sleep, training load, and energy across a 30-day lookback window.
OpenAI Responses API (gpt-5-mini) generates structured readiness narratives with web search and tool routing for nutrition parsing.
Monet pixel art theme with 110+ sprites, four time-of-day palettes (morning/noon/twilight/night), and layered compositing.

title: LecteurAide
oneLiner: Six-stage NLP pipeline using Gemini 1.5 Pro and Cloud Translation that turns French PDFs into scene-segmented reading experiences with sentence-level translation, vocabulary extraction, and comprehension quizzes.
featuredDescription: French reading companion: Gemini-powered scene segmentation, sentence-level translation, vocab extraction, and quizzes.
cardDescription: Gemini-powered French reader with scene segmentation, translation, vocab, and quizzes.
proofPoints: Six-stage pipeline: PDF extraction, heading stripping, sentence segmentation, Cloud Translation, chunking, and Gemini annotation
Context-aware scene segmentation that passes previous scene summaries to maintain narrative coherence across chunks
SSE-streaming upload progress, keyboard-navigable scene reader, and click-to-jump vocabulary highlighting
overview: LecteurAide is a French reading companion for long-form PDFs. It ingests a book, segments it into scenes, translates sentences, extracts vocabulary, and generates comprehension questions before presenting everything in a purpose-built reading interface.
whatIBuilt: FastAPI backend for book upload, scene segmentation, translation, vocabulary extraction, and persistence
Next.js frontend for upload, library browsing, and scene-by-scene reading
Vertex AI and Google Cloud Translation pipeline for turning raw PDFs into guided reading experiences
howItWorks: Upload a French PDF and title via the ingestion API
Segment the text into scenes and annotate it with translations, vocab, and questions
Persist the processed book and render it through the web reader
results: Turns static PDFs into an interactive reading workflow
Combines document ingestion, translation, and reader UX in one end-to-end product
nextSteps: Add screenshots and a richer sample book walkthrough
Expand the reader with stronger review and spaced-repetition loops
bullets: Six-stage FastAPI pipeline: PDF text extraction, sentence segmentation, batch Cloud Translation (150 sentences per call), and Gemini-driven scene annotation.
Context-aware scene segmentation with deduplication, vocabulary grouped by part of speech, and 4 comprehension questions per scene.
Next.js reader with SSE upload streaming, keyboard scene navigation, adjustable text scale, and click-to-sentence vocabulary lookup.

title: Human Digit Recognition
oneLiner: Two-stage PyTorch pipeline (~7.5M params each) that localizes a hand bounding box then classifies raised finger count, trained on a custom annotated dataset.
featuredDescription: Two-stage PyTorch pipeline: hand localization then finger-count classification with a custom dataset and Flask demo.
cardDescription: Two-stage PyTorch CV pipeline: hand detection then finger-count classification.
proofPoints: Two CNNs (~7.5M params each): 4-layer conv stack + 9-layer dense head on 28x28 grayscale inputs
Custom dataset with canvas-based bounding-box annotation tool and albumentations augmentation
Cross-platform inference with automatic CUDA/MPS/CPU device detection
overview: Human Digit Recognition is a two-stage perception project that detects a hand region in an image and then classifies how many digits are raised. It includes the modeling workflow, annotation tooling, pretrained weights, and a lightweight Flask-based demo path.
whatIBuilt: PyTorch models for hand-box detection and digit-count classification
Annotation tooling and notebooks for training and evaluation
Flask inference server and browser-facing prediction flow
howItWorks: Capture or upload an image frame
Predict a hand bounding box from the full frame
Crop the detected region and run digit classification
Return the predicted count through the Flask API
results: End-to-end perception workflow from annotation to serving
Demonstrates staged CV inference rather than a single-model toy demo
nextSteps: Package dependencies more cleanly for reproducible setup
Add screenshots and clearer example outputs to the repo and portfolio
bullets: Stage 1 CNN predicts hand bounding box; stage 2 crops and classifies finger count (0-9) via argmax over 10 logits.
Custom annotation tool (Flask + canvas) and albumentations augmentation pipeline with no external dataset dependency.
End-to-end inference auto-detects CUDA, Apple MPS, or CPU and handles PIL, numpy, or file path inputs.

title: Wildfire Modeling
oneLiner: Multi-language pipeline (Python, JavaScript, R) that downloads GOES satellite imagery via Earth Engine, converts to GOFER-format JSON, and runs locational logistic regressions over fire spread.
cardDescription: Wildfire research pipeline: Earth Engine satellite downloads, GOFER-format JSON normalization, scikit-learn logistic regressions, and a canvas-based fire viewer.
proofPoints: Earth Engine download and normalization pipeline
Locational spread + continuation regression workflows
Lightweight local viewer for GOFER-style JSON outputs
overview: Wildfire Modeling is a research workflow for collecting wildfire-related geospatial data, converting it into GOFER-style JSON, and running local regression analyses over fire spread and continuation. It sits between raw remote-sensing data and downstream modeling experiments.
whatIBuilt: CLI scripts for downloading GOES confidence stacks and aligned RTMA meteorology
Conversion tools that normalize geospatial outputs into modeling-friendly JSON
Regression workflows and notebooks for single-fire and multi-fire analysis
howItWorks: Download wildfire confidence stacks and aligned meteorology
Convert outputs into GOFER-style JSON time series
Run locational regressions and inspect outputs through notebooks or the local viewer
results: Repeatable research workflow from Earth Engine exports to regression outputs
Supports both single-fire analysis and broader multi-fire experiments
nextSteps: Tighten environment bootstrapping and add clearer example outputs
Expand the multiresolution data path and result visualization
bullets: 15 Python CLI scripts, 18 Earth Engine JavaScript modules, and 6 R post-processing scripts across the full pipeline.
Downloads GOES confidence stacks and hourly RTMA meteorology (temp, wind, humidity) with adaptive multi-band chunking.
Canvas-based web viewer with 5-stop color gradient, time-slider navigation, and metadata display for GOFER JSON outputs.

title: Sentiment Market Arb
oneLiner: Arbitrage detection engine with 25+ API endpoints and 12 database models that ingests Kalshi and Polymarket odds, maps events via weighted scoring, and scans for covered-outcome opportunities.
cardDescription: Full-stack arbitrage engine: Kalshi + Polymarket ingestion, weighted-score event mapping, 2-way and 3-way arb scanning, and a React operator console.
proofPoints: Odds normalization across decimal, fractional, and moneyline formats with venue-specific fee models
Weighted event mapper (60% team match, 30% time proximity, 10% league) with auto-event creation fallback
2-way and 3-way arb detection with stake optimization, worst-case ROI filtering, and per-leg breakdown
overview: Sentiment Market Arb is a full-stack prototype for ingesting venue data from prediction markets, normalizing it into a shared sports-event schema, and scanning for pure arbitrage opportunities across covered outcomes.
whatIBuilt: FastAPI backend with ingestion routes, mappings, quote storage, and arbitrage scan endpoints
Postgres-backed schema for events, markets, mapping candidates, quotes, and arbitrage outputs
React operator console for ingestion, mapping review, and arbitrage inspection
howItWorks: Ingest venue markets and quotes from supported platforms
Map venue-specific markets onto canonical sports events
Store normalized quotes and run arbitrage scans across compatible outcome groups
results: Prototype covers ingestion, normalization, review, and scan flows end-to-end
Provides a concrete full-stack environment for arbitrage research rather than a paper design
nextSteps: Tighten secrets/env documentation and add example outputs
Add more robust verification and benchmark flows for scan quality
bullets: 25+ FastAPI endpoints across 7 router groups with a 12-model Postgres schema for venues, events, markets, quotes, mappings, and arbs.
Weighted composite scorer (team match + time proximity + league) maps Kalshi/Polymarket markets to canonical events with auto-creation fallback.
Arb engine solves 2-way stake ratios algebraically and tests 3-way equal-unit heuristics, filtering by worst-case ROI threshold.

title: PokerML
oneLiner: CFR+ solver for heads-up NLHE with k-means card abstraction, external sampling, and a FastAPI web frontend with live 13x13 range visualization.
cardDescription: CFR+ poker solver: 100K iterations of external sampling MCCFR, card bucketing, and a web UI with live range heatmaps.
proofPoints: CFR+ with external sampling: 100K iterations, regret clamping, bucket-cached traversal across all 4 streets
K-means preflop abstraction (10 buckets) + empirical CDF postflop bucketing with sampled board completions
FastAPI web UI: play against the bot with a live 13x13 range heatmap showing action probabilities per hand
overview: PokerML is a heads-up no-limit hold'em solver built on CFR+ (Counterfactual Regret Minimization with regret clamping) and external sampling Monte Carlo. It trains over 100,000 iterations with k-means preflop bucketing and percentile-based postflop abstraction, then serves the converged strategy through a FastAPI web app where you can play against the bot and inspect its 13x13 range grid in real time.
whatIBuilt: CFR+ trainer with external sampling MCCFR, immutable game states, and per-iteration bucket caching for speed
Two-level card abstraction: k-means on preflop equity (169 hands to 10 buckets) and percentile CDF on postflop hand strength
Action abstraction with 50% and 100% pot bet sizings, fold/check/call, all-in, and a 4-raise-per-street cap
FastAPI server with session management, bot inference, and a 13x13 range grid API for the frontend
howItWorks: Deal hands and board, pre-cache card buckets for both players across all 4 streets
Traverse the game tree: enumerate all actions for the traversing player, sample one for the opponent
Update cumulative regrets (clamped to zero for CFR+) and accumulate strategy sums
After 100K iterations, compress the averaged strategy to JSON and serve it via FastAPI for live play
results: Converged strategy across ~1,173 lines of Python with 18.5 MB compressed strategy file
Interactive web UI lets you play full hands and inspect the bot's action distribution for any of 169 canonical hands
nextSteps: Add variance reduction techniques (pruning, warm starting)
Expand action abstraction with more bet sizings and raise depths

coverImage.alt: PokerML preflop range matrix and decision tree

bullets: CFR+ with external sampling: 100K iterations, regret clamping, and per-deal bucket caching across preflop through river.
K-means preflop bucketing (169 hands to 10 clusters) + empirical CDF postflop abstraction with sampled completions.
FastAPI web app with live 13x13 range heatmap: color-coded actions, per-hand drill-down, and bot play sessions.

title: MBTA Leave Now
oneLiner: Spring Boot app that solves multi-leg MBTA commutes by matching trip IDs across lines, computing walk and transfer buffers, and serving leave-by times through a REST API.
cardDescription: Spring Boot commute solver: multi-leg trip matching across MBTA lines with walk/transfer buffers and a leave-by planner UI.
proofPoints: MBTA prediction-driven leave-now API
Fixed commute leave-by planner with transfer and walking buffers
Spring Boot + Gradle Kotlin DSL practice project
overview: MBTA Leave Now is a learning-focused Spring Boot project that answers a commuter question: when should I leave? It includes both a generic stop-level API and a more opinionated leave-by planner for a fixed commute to RTX BBN.
whatIBuilt: Spring MVC endpoints for stop-level leave guidance and a fixed commute planner
MBTA API client using Java HttpClient and Jackson parsing
Minimal browser UI for entering a deadline and viewing leave-by guidance
howItWorks: Fetch upcoming MBTA predictions and schedules for relevant stops
Apply walk-time and transfer buffers to compute a leave-by recommendation
Expose results through a REST API and a lightweight static frontend
results: Concrete practice project for Gradle Kotlin DSL, Spring MVC, and test structure
Bridges backend API logic and a usable frontend in one small repo
nextSteps: Generalize the fixed commute planner into configurable routes
Add example responses and screenshots for easier onboarding
bullets: Fetches real-time MBTA predictions and schedules, filtering by stop, route, and direction with paginated responses.
Multi-leg commute solver matches trip IDs across Orange and Red lines with configurable walk-time and transfer buffers.
Headway bunching detection via coefficient-of-variation scoring to assess service reliability.

title: study-buddy
oneLiner: Agentic study pipeline using CrewAI and ChromaDB that converts PDFs and lecture notes into Anki flashcard decks and difficulty-weighted LaTeX cheat sheets.
cardDescription: Agentic study pipeline: CrewAI agents + ChromaDB RAG turn PDFs into Anki flashcard decks and difficulty-weighted LaTeX cheat sheets.
proofPoints: Document ingestion -> flashcards -> cheat-sheet workflow
RAG-backed study flow using ChromaDB
FastAPI app with a focused single-page interface
overview: study-buddy is a study companion that turns lecture notes, research papers, and Anki exports into curated flashcards, decks, and cheat sheets. It combines LLM tooling, retrieval, and a simple web interface into one study workflow.
whatIBuilt: Built the full ingestion-to-artifact pipeline for study-material processing and generation
FastAPI-backed flow for converting source material into flashcards and cheat sheets
RAG path using ChromaDB to keep generations anchored in the uploaded material
howItWorks: Upload notes, papers, or deck exports
Index material into a retrieval layer
Generate flashcards, exports, and cheat-sheet style outputs from the grounded context
results: Prototype shows a concrete AI-assisted study loop from raw notes to review artifacts
nextSteps: Add richer example outputs and onboarding documentation
Add screenshots and example outputs to support portfolio presentation

coverImage.alt: Study Buddy flashcard and cheat sheet generator

bullets: CrewAI agents (flashcard, cheatsheet, RAG, aggregator) orchestrate a multi-step pipeline from document ingestion to artifact export.
ChromaDB vector store with LangChain embeddings grounds all generation in uploaded source material via similarity search.
Exports Anki .apkg decks and compiles difficulty-weighted LaTeX cheat sheets to PDF, using Anki review history for prioritization.

title: N-Queens Genetic Algorithm
oneLiner: Genetic algorithm that solves N-Queens via tournament selection and adaptive 1/N mutation—finds conflict-free boards for N=8 in under 500 generations.
cardDescription: N-Queens GA solver with tournament selection (k=3), single-point crossover, and 1/N adaptive mutation rate—solves N=8 in ~500 generations.
proofPoints: Tournament selection + single-point crossover
Mutation schedule that scales with board size
CLI/.env configurable experiments
overview: This project explores the N-Queens puzzle through a compact genetic algorithm. It is intentionally small and transparent, making it easy to inspect how population size, mutation behavior, and iteration caps affect convergence.
whatIBuilt: Single-file genetic algorithm implementation with tournament selection and crossover
Collision-based fitness function for scoring candidate boards
CLI and .env configuration for quick experimentation
howItWorks: Initialize a population of candidate boards
Score conflicts and select parents via tournaments
Apply crossover and mutation across generations
Stop when a zero-collision solution is found or iteration cap is hit
results: Good compact reference implementation for an evolutionary search workflow
nextSteps: Add seeded benchmarking and clearer example outputs
Try alternate encodings or hybrid local-search ideas

coverImage.alt: N-Queens genetic algorithm solution with convergence chart

bullets: Tournament selection (k=3) picks two parents; single-point crossover produces offspring; 1/N per-gene mutation adapts to board size.
Population of 200, cap of 200K generations, all configurable via .env or CLI—solves N=8 in under 500 generations.
Collision-counting fitness function runs O(N^2) pairwise checks across rows, columns, and both diagonals.

title: Maze-Game
oneLiner: Java world program that generates mazes via Kruskal's algorithm on weighted grids, animates BFS and DFS solutions frame-by-frame, and supports manual keyboard play.
cardDescription: Java maze generator using Kruskal's on weighted grids with tick-based BFS/DFS animation and an arrow-key manual play mode.
proofPoints: Maze generation via weighted graph + spanning tree ideas
BFS and DFS solver visualization
Interactive player mode with keyboard controls
overview: Maze-Game is a Java world program that generates solvable mazes, animates breadth-first or depth-first search, and supports a manual play mode. It combines graph generation, search visualization, and event-driven interaction in one compact project.
whatIBuilt: Maze generator over a grid graph using weighted edges and union-find style logic
Solver playback for BFS and DFS so pathfinding is visible instead of abstract
Manual mode with movement, collision checks, and win detection
howItWorks: Generate a maze from weighted connections across a grid
Choose BFS or DFS to visualize a solver path
Optionally switch into manual mode and play through the maze
results: Good early project for graph algorithms, search, and interactive Java state
nextSteps: Add screenshots or GIFs of generation and solver playback
Document the course-library setup more precisely

coverImage.alt: Maze generation and BFS solution path

bullets: Kruskal's algorithm with union-find on a weighted grid graph; supports horizontal/vertical edge bias for varied maze shapes.
Tick-based animation renders BFS or DFS search one node per frame (0.01s), then traces the solution path in green.
Manual mode with arrow-key movement, collision validation against maze connections, and a win-state overlay at the exit.

title: Hex Reversi
oneLiner: Hex-grid Reversi in 48 classes with cube-coordinate geometry, a composable AI strategy framework (TryTwo combinator), and a Swing GUI—plus a full adapter layer for third-party provider integration.
cardDescription: Hex-grid Reversi: 48-class MVC architecture with cube coordinates, composable AI strategies via TryTwo combinator, and adapter-pattern provider integration.
proofPoints: Cube-coordinate hex board (q+r+s=0) with odd-r offset conversion and configurable side length
TryTwo combinator chains strategies (corners → avoid-near-corners → minimax → capture-max) with fallible/infallible pattern
Four adapter classes bridge third-party provider code without modifying core—ReadOnly model, view, input, and strategy adapters
overview: Hex Reversi is an extensible Reversi/Othello implementation on a hexagonal grid. The core emphasis is software design: clean MVC separation, adapters for different play modes, and modular AI strategies that can be mixed and matched.
whatIBuilt: Hex-grid Reversi engine with configurable board shapes and starting states
MVC architecture with GUI + text views and a controller layer
Strategy system including minimax and composable heuristics
howItWorks: Represent the board on an odd-r hex coordinate system
Validate moves and apply flips according to Reversi rules
Run players (human/AI) through a controller loop
Plug different strategies into the AI player
nextSteps: Add stronger evaluation heuristics and time controls
Improve UI polish and strategy visualization
bullets: 48-class MVC architecture with cube-coordinate hex board, observer-driven controller, and Swing GUI with hexagon rendering and move previewing.
TryTwo combinator composes four strategies (corner capture, corner avoidance, 2-ply minimax, max capture) with fallible/infallible type safety.
Adapter layer (4 classes, ~600 LOC) integrates third-party provider code without touching core implementation—Strategy, Observer, Factory, and Decorator patterns throughout.

title: Linux Shell (C)
oneLiner: 793-line C shell with recursive arbitrary-depth piping, I/O redirection, 5 built-ins, and a custom dynamic vector for tokenization.
cardDescription: Custom C shell in 793 lines: recursive arbitrary-depth piping, input/output redirection, tokenizer with quote handling, and 5 built-in commands.
proofPoints: Recursive pipe chaining supports arbitrary-depth pipelines via right-to-left parsing and fork/dup2 orchestration
5 built-ins (cd, source, prev, help, verbose) communicate with parent via a dedicated global IPC pipe
Single-pass tokenizer handles quoted strings, special characters, and whitespace with a custom 2x-growth dynamic vector
overview: A custom shell written in C with support for command execution, piping, redirection, and signals. The project focuses on process control and correctness under typical shell behaviors.
whatIBuilt: Command execution with piping and I/O redirection
Signal handling and process orchestration
Built-in commands (source, prev, cd, help, verbose toggle)
howItWorks: Parse input into commands and pipeline segments
Fork/exec child processes and wire pipes between them
Apply redirections and handle signals appropriately
Track history for built-ins like prev
nextSteps: Add job control (fg/bg) if desired
Harden parsing for edge cases and quoting rules
bullets: Recursive pipe architecture: right-to-left parsing paired with recursive fork/dup2 elegantly chains arbitrary-depth pipelines.
6-layer execution stack (command lines → pipes → redirects → fork/exec) with global IPC pipe for built-in cd/source communication.
Custom dynamic vector (2x growth, string-copying) backs a single-pass tokenizer that handles quotes, special chars, and delimiters.
